title: Cat Squinting in One Eye: Causes, Care, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat squinting in one eye? Learn about common causes, when it's an emergency, and how a photo helps your vet triage the issue effectively.
content_html:
TL;DR: Squinting in one eye is often a sign of mild irritation that can be monitored for 12–24 hours, but immediate veterinary care is needed if you notice cloudiness or changes in pupil size.

What does it mean if my cat is squinting in one eye?

When a cat squints in one eye, it is usually a sign of ocular discomfort or blepharospasm. This occurs when the muscles around the eye involuntary contract to protect the globe from irritation. Common causes include minor environmental irritants like dust, mild allergies, or a small scratch on the eyelid. While it indicates discomfort, it is often the body's natural way of protecting the eye from further light or debris.

Is it an emergency if my cat is squinting in one eye?

  • If your cat is eating, playing, and acting normally, you can typically monitor the situation for 12 to 24 hours.
  • Contact a veterinarian immediately if you notice the eye becoming cloudy.
  • Seek veterinary care if there is a visible change in the pupil size.
  • Consult a professional if your cat is constantly pawing at their face, as this may indicate a corneal ulcer.

How does taking a photo of my cat's eye help with veterinary triage?

  • Capturing a high-resolution photo allows a professional to see the exact degree of squinting.
  • Photos help identify the color of the conjunctiva and any subtle discharge not easily seen in person.
  • Visual evidence assists the veterinary team in determining if an immediate visit is required or if home monitoring is safe.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, squinting in one eye (blepharospasm) can be associated with various ocular and systemic diseases. Unilateral conjunctivitis, potentially causing squinting, may result from a foreign body, dacryocystitis, or keratoconjunctivitis sicca. Feline herpesvirus-1 (FHV-1), Mycoplasma, or Chlamydia psittaci can cause conjunctivitis that starts unilaterally and becomes bilateral within a week. Other causes of ocular discomfort include infectious agents (bacteria, viruses, mycoplasma, fungi, and parasites) and environmental irritants. Systemic diseases, such as feline infectious peritonitis, toxoplasmosis, feline immunodeficiency virus, and feline leukemia virus, can manifest as anterior and posterior uveitis, potentially leading to squinting and other ocular signs. Specific diagnosis requires a thorough history, physical examination, conjunctival scrapings and culture, and Schirmer tear test.

Chapter: Oncology, Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 518)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, never use human eye drops or medications on your cat without a veterinarian's approval, as many contain ingredients that can cause severe irritation or even permanent damage to feline eyes.

faqs.question: How should I clean my cat's squinting eye?
faqs.answer: If there is mild discharge, you can gently wipe the area around the eye with a clean, soft cloth dampened with lukewarm water. Avoid touching the surface of the eye itself.

faqs.question: What are the signs that a squinting eye is becoming serious?
faqs.answer: Seek veterinary care if the eye appears sunken, turns red or cloudy, or if your cat begins showing signs of lethargy or loss of appetite.
